item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overly long for the story that it tries to tell, and not particularly engaging. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe they're also anomalously stereotypical?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does that count as MILDLY hostile, exactly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe that's part of the anomaly here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the humour. A hippie, gluten-free witch, an area in which vaccinations cause autism and homeopathy works, it was great. Until the end, all the [DATA EXPUNGED] ruined it for me and I feel like it didn't fit the tone of the article at all.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to know how cognizant they are of the fact that nothing they do works outside their range.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is hilarious. I managed to hold it in until I got to the vaccination bit, but then I just broke out laughing. The skip is well done, the concept's strikingly believable (in context, heh), and you even got the botanical names right. (I did go through and fix a bit of capitalization in the common names, noted that Cedrus libani is specifically Lebanon cedar, and fixed the spelling of Ilex aquifolium, but that's just details.)

+1. This is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely hate -B. Like, not as an SCP; the whole thing works well. I just dislike -B's personality. I hate conspiracy theorists with a passion. There's a Tale in the Broken Masquerade canon about conspiracy theorists, and I wanted to punch the characters in the mouth. Someone I know recently said that Ebola was created by the government, and it was at that point I quit having respect for them. Conspiracy theorists are my bane. I just hate when people ignore the information readily available to them and believe utter bullshit instead. I suppose this also applies to -A, but there were plenty of people who believed that sort of stuff centuries ago, and no simple way to disprove it, especially to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is amazing. The mental image of their fight totally sold this for me.

+ There are somethings that needs a tweak
- Like... these:

SCP-2164-B spent its life before Foundation recovery in Orange County, California

"Spent its life"
Like… how? or where? This might need some specification, at least to me.

reëstabilshed

I'm tempted to say that the guy that wrote the SCP article was somehow influenced by -A, but I'm not so sure about this :P

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having recently been exposed to a "they know the cure for cancer but they're covering it up because it would destroy drug company profits" person…this is genius. Upvoted with pleasure and a wave of healing crystals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Vaccinated people become autistic if they're too close to her. Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. He's old and stubborn and thinks B is an idiot, so I didn't think he'd update his dialect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ding ding ding! He sure is! I didn't know if anyone would catch on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is D-80044 an Indigo Child?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assumed that he talked that way just to be pretentious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It then administered mL of the diluted solution to D-99746,

how many mL?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I'll fix it up accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bah! May the Gnawer of Corpses feast on the bones of these agents and vultures in league with the White-Christ! Those who would use the secrets of the Eighteen Charms, bequeath to us by the Drinker of the Well, without also giving him his due?! To Nastrond they shall be sent!

/endNorseShamanRant

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one thing keeping me from upvote is the plethora of logs. After one or two showing their effects and personalities, I think we get the point. The interview helps drive home their feelings toward each other. I do suggest making it clearer that each person has their own field effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The event log at the bottom was clever and had me chuckling, but I just lost it when they charged at each other in a ritualistic manner. That mental image. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd been mulling over how to do a "new age magic actually works… sometimes" SCP for a while, and I think that you knocked it out of the park here. Contrasting it with alchemy was a great idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, if this guy is an immortal, his dialect probably would have kept up with the times, so there's really no need for old english in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooookay. Well, I like the overall idea, of a field in which magic works. I like -A a lot, it seems well-thought out and interesting. I do not at all like your attitude towards -B, which seems full of lazy stereotypes (O.C. California? Really?) and a sneering attitude.

I also think it's far too long. I got bored reading through the descriptions of -B's tests, as they were all basically the same. I did like that you can't bring people with a high IQ near her, because I couldn't figure out why that might be, and that was intriguing. But that was about all I liked about -B.

My suggestion: get rid of -B, explore -A's attitude a little more. Where does he think he is? What has he been doing for hundreds of years? How was he contained initially? Some hint of his personality (beyond a generic 'I am warlock I have power' cliché) would be very nice.

Oh, and the Middle English is essentially pointless. I respect that you've put effort into it, but think about this from a Foundation point of view. How is it in any way useful to them to have untranslated text in an entirely different language to the rest in the middle of documentation? 99% of the readers of this file aren't going to understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oi, I remember this one when it was in the forums. Always loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay you guys want Middle English? You get your Middle English

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Each has their own field, and they're not supposed to enter each other's. I did draw on my limited knowledge of old English as much as I could, but I would appreciate more help polishing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never mind, I snuck in and fixed them.
My brain told me to go do it, and I was like, "OK brain, let's do it!"
:D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty well-written and original. I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it; The new age vs old age thing is clever, the dialog in the incident log is fun, and it's all around excellent writing. This could have slid easily into -J territory, but it's been done well as a serious SCP. +1

2 little notes:

The 'Tests Involving SCP-2164-B' section, Test 2, last line, "SCP-XXXX-B refused to elaborate further."
Just need to replace that XXXX

Also, in the closing statement - The italicizing '//' at the begin and end are visible, and did not italicize that part. Not exactly sure why not, but shouldn't be too tricky to fix.

Those are the only things I saw wrong with the entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea behind this, but the execution just seems a little off. The nature of the "magic field" isn't fully explained (do they each have their own, or are they connected in some way?). I didn't like that last log in particular because of the "old english" -A spoke and the clumsy expungement at the end. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to 19, especially BlackWing, Deveraux, and especially Zyn for adding that rating module I always forget >.>
